items.snippet: Runtime & HTTP Tokio - async runtime, the concurrency foundation. async-std was skipped due to weaker ecosystem support. Axum - HTTP routing with strong type safety, sits naturally on Tower. actix-web was ruled out for being more actor-oriented and less aligned here. Tower / tower-http / tower_governor - middleware as composable layers: CORS, tracing, request IDs, and rate limiting all live here, outside business logic where they belong.
items.searchText: application stack core stack at a glance  runtime runtime & http tokio - async runtime, the concurrency foundation. async-std was skipped due to weaker ecosystem support. axum - http routing with strong type safety, sits naturally on tower. actix-web was ruled out for being more actor-oriented and less aligned here. tower / tower-http / tower_governor - middleware as composable layers: cors, tracing, request ids, and rate limiting all live here, outside business logic where they belong.

items.snippet: Data & messaging SQLx - async PostgreSQL with compile-time query validation. Diesel's DSL felt too restrictive for the fine-tuned SQL this system needs. Lapin - AMQP client for RabbitMQ. Chosen for fine-grained control; higher-level wrappers hide too much. Redis - idempotency checks and distributed locks. Fast coordination layer between workers and the API.
items.searchText: application stack core stack at a glance  data data & messaging sqlx - async postgresql with compile-time query validation. diesel's dsl felt too restrictive for the fine-tuned sql this system needs. lapin - amqp client for rabbitmq. chosen for fine-grained control; higher-level wrappers hide too much. redis - idempotency checks and distributed locks. fast coordination layer between workers and the api.

items.snippet: Serialization & validation Serde + serde_json - standard, mature, no real alternative in Rust. Validator - declarative rules on DTOs, enforced before any core logic runs. phonenumber - international phone validation. Regex simply isn't reliable for global numbering rules. UUID + Chrono - non-sequential IDs and consistent UTC timestamps throughout.
items.searchText: application stack core stack at a glance  serialization serialization & validation serde + serde_json - standard, mature, no real alternative in rust. validator - declarative rules on dtos, enforced before any core logic runs. phonenumber - international phone validation. regex simply isn't reliable for global numbering rules. uuid + chrono - non-sequential ids and consistent utc timestamps throughout.

items.snippet: Observability & config Tracing + tracing-subscriber - structured spans that survive async boundaries, formatted as JSON for observability pipelines. Envy + dotenvy - typed config from environment variables, fails fast on missing values. dotenvy replaces the unmaintained dotenv . Secrecy - wraps sensitive values to prevent accidental exposure in logs or memory.
items.searchText: application stack core stack at a glance  observability observability & config tracing + tracing-subscriber - structured spans that survive async boundaries, formatted as json for observability pipelines. envy + dotenvy - typed config from environment variables, fails fast on missing values. dotenvy replaces the unmaintained dotenv . secrecy - wraps sensitive values to prevent accidental exposure in logs or memory.

items.snippet: Security & reliability thiserror + anyhow - structured errors for domain logic, flexible errors for worker flows. subtle - constant-time comparisons to prevent timing attacks on sensitive checks. rand - secure token generation and retry jitter. futures-util - stream and async utilities the standard library doesn't cover yet.
items.searchText: application stack core stack at a glance  security security & reliability thiserror + anyhow - structured errors for domain logic, flexible errors for worker flows. subtle - constant-time comparisons to prevent timing attacks on sensitive checks. rand - secure token generation and retry jitter. futures-util - stream and async utilities the standard library doesn't cover yet.

items.snippet: API docs Utoipa + utoipa-swagger-ui - OpenAPI spec generated at compile time, served as an interactive Swagger UI. Manual Swagger files drift; this keeps docs and code in sync automatically.
items.searchText: application stack core stack at a glance  api-docs api docs utoipa + utoipa-swagger-ui - openapi spec generated at compile time, served as an interactive swagger ui. manual swagger files drift; this keeps docs and code in sync automatically.

items.snippet: High-level architecture Preview - opens full diagram in Google Drive Click to view Architecture diagram (Google Drive) Three workers. One clear job each Before anything else - this system runs exactly three background workers, and none of them overlap in responsibility: Outbox relay Bridges Postgres to RabbitMQ Stage processor Consumes from RabbitMQ and drives verification logic Lease recovery Cleans up crashed jobs, runs entirely out-of-band That's the foundation.</p

items.snippet: API architecture Foundation: one process, many concurrent requests Before diving into the layers - the API is a single OS process running a Tokio async runtime. There are no threads per request, no forking, no copying of state. Tokio multiplexes thousands of concurrent connections across a small fixed thread pool. Everything below is built on top of that single fact. The HTTP engine - Hyper & Axum Axum is not a heavy framework. It's a thin routing layer on top of Hyper, which handles the raw TCP byte stream and HTTP header parsing. Every route is a tower::Service - a typed async function from Request to Response. Routing a request to a handler is a pointer lookup, nothing more. No reflection…

items.snippet: Request contract URL design - resource-oriented slugs POST /v1/verify/{session_id}/stages/{stage_name} Every segment is deliberate. /v1/ is explicit versioning - when a v2 flow ships with 10 stages, both versions run concurrently without breaking existing clients. {session_id} in the path rather than the body lets the router and log infrastructure (CloudWatch) index by session ID without parsing JSON payloads. Request contract - zero trust at the border The principle: assume every incoming request is malformed or malicious until the type system proves otherwise. Business logic never sees bad data. ValidatedJson<T> runs a two-pass check before the handler wakes up. The first pass is structura…

items.snippet: Response contract Predictable by design Every response from this API, success or failure, follows a fixed shape. Clients never have to guess what they'll get back. Success (201/202) { "session_id": "uuid", "status": "PENDING", "message": "Human readable hint" } Error (4xx/5xx) { "error": "validation_failed", "message": "One or more fields failed validation", "trace_id": "016x_hex_string", "details": { "phone": "Invalid format" } } trace_id on every error. When a frontend engineer reports a 500, you don't ask for a screenshot - you ask for the trace_id . Paste it into your logs and you get the exact database queries, Redis checks, and RabbitMQ interactions for that specific request, down to t…

items.snippet: Worker architecture Foundation: workers are not threads Before the layers - each worker is a single long-running Tokio async task, not a thread pool spinning up per job. There's no thread-per-message model here. Tokio's event loop cooperatively schedules everything, and the primitives below are what make that safe, concurrent, and survivable under real infrastructure pressure. The async engine - Tokio & JoinSets Each worker runs an infinite loop {} inside a Tokio task. When the Stage Processor pulls multiple messages, it doesn't process them one-by-one, nor does it blindly tokio::spawn (which leaks if tasks hang). Every message becomes a future pushed into a tokio::task::JoinSet . The set fi…

items.snippet: Data architecture Preview - opens full diagram in Google Drive Click to view ERD diagram (Google Drive) verification_sessions table Tracks the top-level lifecycle of a verification attempt. UUID primary key prevents enumeration. status is the terminal state machine: ACTIVE, COMPLETED, or FAILED. completed_at and failed_at are separate nullable columns - not a single ended_at - because how it ended matters. updated_at is trigger-managed, always accurate. idx_sessions_status covers any sweep filtering by status. verification_stages</c

items.snippet: Message queue architecture Foundation: three tiers, one rule - no message is ever lost Preview - opens full diagram in Google Drive Click to view Queue topology diagram (Google Drive) Every message in this system has exactly three possible fates: it succeeds, it retries, or it lands in a graveyard for human inspection. There is no fourth option where it silently disappears. The publishers Messages are published with delivery mode 2 - persistent. RabbitMQ writes them to disk before acknowledging. A broker crash or restart does not lose in-flight messages.
items.searchText: architecture message queue architecture  message queue architecture message queue architecture foundation: three tiers, one rule - no message is ever lost preview - opens full diagram in google drive click to view queue topology diagram (google drive) every message in this system has exactly three possible fates: it succeeds, it retries, or it lands in a graveyard for human inspection. there is no fourth option where it silently disappears. the publishers messages are published with delivery mode 2 - persistent. rabbitmq writes them to disk before acknowledging. a broker crash or restart does not lose in-flight messages.

items.snippet: Runtime ENVIRONMENT - Switches log format. local gives readable pretty logs; anything else outputs compact JSON for log pipelines. Don't run local in production - it's verbose and expensive to ingest. SERVER_PORT - TCP port the API binds to. Exists so infrastructure can remap ports without touching code. RATE_LIMIT_REQUESTS_PER_MIN - Per-IP request cap enforced in memory. Increase during expected traffic spikes to reduce false 429s. Decrease during an active attack to throttle an abusive IP faster. CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS - Empty disables CORS entirely. Populated values are parsed at boot - a typo crashes the app immediately rather than silently breaking browser traffic in production.
items.searchText: configuration runtime  runtime runtime environment - switches log format. local gives readable pretty logs; anything else outputs compact json for log pipelines. don't run local in production - it's verbose and expensive to ingest. server_port - tcp port the api binds to. exists so infrastructure can remap ports without touching code. rate_limit_requests_per_min - per-ip request cap enforced in memory. increase during expected traffic spikes to reduce false 429s. decrease during an active attack to throttle an abusive ip faster. cors_allowed_origins - empty disables cors entirely. populated values are parsed at boot - a typo crashes the app immediately rather than silently breaking browser traffic in production.

items.snippet: PostgreSQL - Main Pool DB_POOL_MIN / MAX - Min keeps warm connections ready; max caps total connections from this process. With 50 API pods at max 20, you're using 1,000 connections - that hits Postgres's global limit fast. Increase max for more parallelism; Decrease if running many replicas against one DB. DB_POOL_ACQUIRE_MS - How long a request waits for a free connection before returning a 500. Too high and requests pile up in RAM until the pod OOMs. Too low and you get premature 500s under short spikes. DB_POOL_IDLE_MS - How long idle connections are kept before being closed. Increase to reduce reconnect churn; Decrease to release Postgres sessions faster on quiet nodes.
items.searchText: configuration postgresql - main pool  postgresql - main pool postgresql - main pool db_pool_min / max - min keeps warm connections ready; max caps total connections from this process. with 50 api pods at max 20, you're using 1,000 connections - that hits postgres's global limit fast. increase max for more parallelism; decrease if running many replicas against one db. db_pool_acquire_ms - how long a request waits for a free connection before returning a 500. too high and requests pile up in ram until the pod ooms. too low and you get premature 500s under short spikes. db_pool_idle_ms - how long idle connections are kept before being closed. increase to reduce reconnect churn; decrease to release postgres sessions faster on quiet nodes.

items.snippet: Outbox relay RELAY_DB_POOL_MIN / MAX - Completely isolated from the main pool. A hung RabbitMQ connection can only exhaust these 5 - the API and processors keep running. Increase if the relay needs more parallel throughput; Decrease to minimize relay's footprint. OUTBOX_RELAY_BATCH_LIMIT - Rows claimed per relay transaction. Increase to clear backlogs faster with fewer round-trips. Decrease to shorten how long row locks are held per cycle. OUTBOX_RELAY_PUBLISH_TIMEOUT_MS - Hard timeout per RabbitMQ publish. The relay holds Postgres row locks while publishing - a hung broker would freeze those locks indefinitely without this. Increase to tolerate slower brokers; Decrease to release locks fast…

items.snippet: Stage processor PROCESSING_LEASE_BUFFER_SECS - Added to the calculated work time to set expires_at . Increase to prevent the recovery worker from stealing jobs that are still legitimately running. Decrease and leases expire prematurely, causing unnecessary recoveries. STAGE_SIMULATED_WORK_SECS_MIN / MAX - Local testing knobs only. Set both to 1-5 seconds for fast end-to-end runs. Leave high to stress-test lease and retry behavior. STAGE_PROCESSING_MAX_RETRIES - Controls three things: RabbitMQ retry routing, DB retry count, and lease recovery quarantine. Increase to give more attempts before permanent failure. Decrease to quarantine bad jobs sooner. STAGE_PROCESSOR_QOS_PREFETCH - How many una…

items.snippet: Lease recovery STAGE_LEASE_RECOVERY_POLL_MS - How often the recovery worker scans for expired leases. Increase to reduce DB load when the system is healthy. Decrease to detect crashed jobs faster. STAGE_LEASE_RECOVERY_BATCH_LIMIT - Recovery rows per transaction. Increase to clear a mass failure faster with fewer transactions. Decrease to keep lock contention low under recovery. WORKER_START_JITTER_MAX_MS - Random delay before first poll on boot. Increase to spread load across more pods. Set to 0 to remove jitter entirely for predictable cold starts.
items.searchText: configuration lease recovery  lease recovery lease recovery stage_lease_recovery_poll_ms - how often the recovery worker scans for expired leases. increase to reduce db load when the system is healthy. decrease to detect crashed jobs faster. stage_lease_recovery_batch_limit - recovery rows per transaction. increase to clear a mass failure faster with fewer transactions. decrease to keep lock contention low under recovery. worker_start_jitter_max_ms - random delay before first poll on boot. increase to spread load across more pods. set to 0 to remove jitter entirely for predictable cold starts.

items.snippet: Infrastructure AMQP_URL / REDIS_URL / API_KEY - No defaults. Missing values crash the app at boot - intentionally. Silent misconfiguration in production is worse than a loud startup failure. IDEMPOTENCY_TTL_SECS - How long a session-start idempotency key lives in Redis. Increase to extend the safe client retry window. Decrease to expire keys sooner and reduce Redis memory usage. RUST_LOG - Log verbosity dial. info keeps costs down. Inject debug or trace per crate for deep troubleshooting without redeployment.
items.searchText: configuration infrastructure  infrastructure infrastructure amqp_url / redis_url / api_key - no defaults. missing values crash the app at boot - intentionally. silent misconfiguration in production is worse than a loud startup failure. idempotency_ttl_secs - how long a session-start idempotency key lives in redis. increase to extend the safe client retry window. decrease to expire keys sooner and reduce redis memory usage. rust_log - log verbosity dial. info keeps costs down. inject debug or trace per crate for deep troubleshooting without redeployment.

items.snippet: API & worker images Both use a multi-stage cargo-chef build. Chef separates dependency compilation from application compilation into distinct Docker layers. The first build is slow - all 200+ dependencies compile from scratch. Every subsequent build skips that layer entirely and only recompiles changed application code. A code change that previously took 10 minutes now takes roughly 10 seconds. The build base is pinned to bookworm to match the runtime image ( distroless/cc-debian12 ). Mismatched glibc versions between build and runtime cause immediate crashes - a binary compiled against a newer glibc simply won't run in the older distroless container. The versions are locked together deliber…

items.snippet: Migrations image Migrations run in a dedicated ephemeral container, not inside the API startup code. Ten API pods booting simultaneously and all trying to migrate would deadlock each other. The migration container runs first, exits with code 0, and only then does the API rollout begin. sqlx-cli is compiled against musl and statically linked, then dropped into a plain Alpine image. No dynamic library dependencies. ca-certificates is installed explicitly - without it, the binary can't verify SSL certificates against managed cloud databases like RDS or Supabase. Only the Postgres feature is compiled in - no MySQL or SQLite drivers bloating the binary. Final image is around 30MB.
items.searchText: devops migrations image  migrations image migrations image migrations run in a dedicated ephemeral container, not inside the api startup code. ten api pods booting simultaneously and all trying to migrate would deadlock each other. the migration container runs first, exits with code 0, and only then does the api rollout begin. sqlx-cli is compiled against musl and statically linked, then dropped into a plain alpine image. no dynamic library dependencies. ca-certificates is installed explicitly - without it, the binary can't verify ssl certificates against managed cloud databases like rds or supabase. only the postgres feature is compiled in - no mysql or sqlite drivers bloating the binary. final image is around 30mb.

items.snippet: Test image cargo test --no-run runs during the image build, forcing all test binaries to compile and bake into the Docker layer. When the tester container runs in CI, tests execute immediately - no compilation wait at test time. The final stage retains the full Rust toolchain so the container can be used interactively during development.

items.snippet: API execution The API layer - what it does and how it flows The API is a gateway. It validates, locks data into Postgres, and hands off to workers. It does no KYC work itself. 1. Every request passes through security before routing The x-api-key header is checked using constant-time comparison - no timing attacks. If the request carries an Idempotency-Key , Redis is checked first. A duplicate request never reaches Postgres - the cached response is returned immediately. Rate limiting is enforced in memory before any database or business logic is touched. 2. All incoming JSON goes through a two-pass validation Serde validates structure first - wrong types are rejected immediately. The validato…

items.snippet: Outbox relay worker What it does and how it flows Single mission: move rows from outbox_events into RabbitMQ without losing a single one. Isolated connection pool The relay uses its own dedicated Postgres pool, separate from the main application pool. A RabbitMQ outage that causes connections to hang can only exhaust the relay's pool - the API and stage processors keep running on the main pool, unaffected. Polling with SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED The worker polls outbox_events in batches of 50 (configurable). SKIP LOCKED means multiple relay pods can poll the same table simultaneously without colliding - any row locked by one pod is instantly skipped by the others. Pods never wait on each …

items.snippet: Lease recovery worker What it does and how it flows Single mission: find jobs that were claimed but never finished, and either resurrect them or permanently bury them. Search and rescue - every 60 seconds (configurable) The worker polls for stages where status = PROCESSING and expires_at is in the past. A worker that claimed a job sets a 120-second lease - if that time has passed, the pod is definitively dead. Rows are fetched in batches of 25 using SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED . Small batches mean a mass node failure dropping thousands of jobs doesn't lock the entire table at once - recovery happens in surgical chunks. Reclaim - reset and re-queue For each expired row, the worker resets th…

items.snippet: Stage processor worker What it does and how it flows The muscle of the engine. Consumes jobs from RabbitMQ, runs the verification logic, drives the stage chain forward, and handles every failure path without losing data. Intake - triage before anything else The worker is configured with QoS prefetch of 1 (configurable) - RabbitMQ only hands it one message at a time per thread. No memory floods from large backlogs. The moment a message arrives, it's triaged immediately. If the payload is missing event_id , session_id , or stage , the message is rejected with requeue: false and sent straight to the dead-letter queue. Garbage is never retried. Claim - idempotency and lease The worker tries to c…

items.snippet: End-to-end walkthrough Preview - opens full diagram in Google Drive Click to view End-to-end sequence diagram (Google Drive) Phase 1 - Session start ( POST /session/start ) The API key is checked in constant time. If an idempotency key is present, Redis is checked first - a duplicate returns the cached response immediately. Otherwise, one transaction opens: one session row ( ACTIVE ) and five stage rows ( LOCKED ) are inserted together. Transaction commits, result is cached in Redis for 2 hours, 201 Created returned with the session UUID. Phase 2 - Stage submission ( POST /stages/{name}<

items.snippet: Indexing - performance decisions and tradeoffs Partial indexes over full indexes The core decision: only index rows that are actively being worked on. idx_stages_polling covers PENDING and PROCESSING rows only. idx_stages_processing_expires_at covers PROCESSING rows with a non-null expires_at only. The tradeoff is deliberate - historical COMPLETED rows, which will be the vast majority at scale, are excluded entirely. A standard index on status would grow with every verification ever processed, eventually becoming slow and memory-hungry. The performance gain - partial indexes stay small and fit in RAM regardless of how large the table grows. Worker query time stays flat instead of degrading a…

items.snippet: UUIDv4 - performance and security tradeoffs What we gained Performance gain - UUIDs are generated in memory with Uuid::new_v4() ; no DB round-trip. Multi-stage transactions do not stall waiting for an ID. Sequential IDs under concurrency trade off serial contention - every insert waits on a locked sequence. Performance gain - global uniqueness without coordination across API/worker pods or shards. Security gain - 122 bits of entropy makes the session ID act as a bearer token; sequential IDs make enumeration trivial. What we gave up Tradeoff - 16 bytes per key vs 8, and more B-tree fragmentation than sequential keys because inserts land at random positions (more page splits, higher index main…

items.sectionTitle: Transaction flows - performance decisions and tradeoffs
items.snippet: Transaction flows - performance decisions and tradeoffs Session creation - one transaction, bulk insert The session row and all five stage rows are written in one transaction using unnest (one SQL statement vs five round-trips). Performance gain - one network round-trip to Postgres regardless of stage count. Tradeoff - slightly larger write transaction; lock time stays small, and latency drops materially . Stage submission - conditional outbox inside one transaction The LOCKED → PENDING update, dependency check, and outbox insert share one transaction scope - no second connection, no race between read and insert. Tradeoff - transaction stays open slightly longer for the conditional read. Per…

items.sectionTitle: Timeout decisions - performance impact and tradeoffs
items.snippet: Timeout decisions - performance impact and tradeoffs Publish timeout (50ms, outbox relay) While publishing, the relay holds row locks. Performance gain - 50ms cap releases locks if the broker hangs, so Postgres stays usable. Tradeoff - a slow-but-healthy broker may be skipped and retried on the next poll; DB availability is prioritized over single-attempt publish speed. Pool acquire timeout (10 seconds, API) Performance gain - requests stop waiting before they pile up in RAM and OOM the pod. Tradeoff - some callers see fast 500s instead of eventually succeeding. Worker database transaction timeout (10 seconds) Long transactions hold row locks. Performance gain - hard ceiling forces nack/requ…

items.sectionTitle: Failure handling - performance decisions and tradeoffs
items.snippet: Failure handling - performance decisions and tradeoffs Retry limits with a hard ceiling - tradeoff: some jobs that might eventually succeed are abandoned. Performance gain: no infinite retry loops eating workers and DB connections. 30-second delay queue - tradeoff: slower end-to-end retry. Performance gain: a bad job cannot tight-loop through the worker at full speed. Reject to dead-letter - tradeoff: requires human replay from the DLQ. Performance gain: main queue stays healthy; poison messages leave the hot path. Lease recovery in small batches - tradeoff: slower full recovery after mass failure. Performance gain: Postgres stays respo

items.sectionTitle: Retry limits - performance decisions and tradeoffs
items.snippet: Retry limits - performance decisions and tradeoffs Stage processing retries (limit: 3) Tradeoff - more retries help transient third-party blips; fewer retries evict toxic work sooner. At 3, a failed job spends ~90s in retry holds before quarantine. Performance gain - beyond that point, expected value of another attempt drops vs resource cost. The 30-second retry delay Performance gain - backpressure: sustained downstream failure cannot flood the main queue with the same failing job. Tradeoff - legitimate recovery waits longer between attempts. Lease recovery retries (limit: 3) Tradeoff - higher limits chase flaky infra longer; lower limits quarantine poison payloads faster. At 3, worst-case …

items.snippet: Edge security API key authentication uses constant-time comparison, not standard string equality. Standard comparison fails the moment it hits a wrong character - measurable response time differences let attackers guess keys byte by byte. Constant-time comparison takes identical time regardless of where the mismatch occurs, eliminating the attack vector entirely. Idempotency keys backed by Redis mean duplicate session-start requests - whether from a glitchy network or a deliberate flood - return the cached response. The database sees exactly one write regardless of how many requests arrive. Rate limits and CORS origins are environment variables. Under an active attack, both can be tightened …

items.snippet: Payload defense ValidatedJson enforces a two-phase gate before any handler runs. Serde validates structure first - wrong types are rejected. The validator crate then enforces field rules: string length caps prevent memory exhaustion, strict enums reject anything outside the allowed set. Phone numbers go through libphonenumber - regex is not sufficient for international formats. If a crafted payload crashes the worker, the lease recovery system tracks it. After three crashes, the payload is permanently quarantined as FAILED and removed from active processing. The cluster is protected from infinite crash loops.
items.searchText: security payload defense  payload defense payload defense validatedjson enforces a two-phase gate before any handler runs. serde validates structure first - wrong types are rejected. the validator crate then enforces field rules: string length caps prevent memory exhaustion, strict enums reject anything outside the allowed set. phone numbers go through libphonenumber - regex is not sufficient for international formats. if a crafted payload crashes the worker, the lease recovery system tracks it. after three crashes, the payload is permanently quarantined as failed and removed from active processing. the cluster is protected from infinite crash loops.

items.snippet: Database security UUID primary keys carry 122 bits of entropy - sequential IDs make enumeration trivial, UUIDs make it computationally impossible. No user_id is stored anywhere in the verification tables. If the database is breached, the attacker gets fragmented verification data with no way to link it to real user accounts. The session UUID is the only credential. All SQL uses parameterized queries verified at compile time against the actual schema. SQL injection is not possible - there is no dynamic query construction. input_data is written once by the API, response_data written once by the worker. Neither can be modified after the fact. The audit trail is structurally immutable.

items.snippet: Container hardening Production images are built on distroless base - no shell, no package manager, no system utilities. Remote code execution via a zero-day lands an attacker in a container with nothing to work with. The Rust binary runs as PID 1 directly. Database migrations run in a separate short-lived Alpine container that exits immediately after applying the schema. The API and worker pods have no schema-altering privileges and no migration code.
items.searchText: security container hardening  container hardening container hardening production images are built on distroless base - no shell, no package manager, no system utilities. remote code execution via a zero-day lands an attacker in a container with nothing to work with. the rust binary runs as pid 1 directly. database migrations run in a separate short-lived alpine container that exits immediately after applying the schema. the api and worker pods have no schema-altering privileges and no migration code.

items.snippet: Leak prevention All secrets - database URL, Redis URL, AMQP URL, API key - are wrapped in secrecy::SecretString . Accidentally printing the config struct outputs [REDACTED] . Secrets cannot reach logs regardless of developer error. Internal 500 errors log the full technical detail internally. The client receives only {"error": "internal_error", "message": "An unexpected error occurred", "trace_id": "..."} . Database schema names, table structures, and stack traces never leave the server.

items.sectionTitle: Foundation: three testing worlds, three purposes
items.snippet: Foundation: three testing worlds, three purposes The test suite is divided into three completely separate environments. Each exists for a different reason and tests a different level of the system. Unit tests run entirely in memory. No database, no Redis, no RabbitMQ. They test pure logic - config parsing, error mapping, validation boundaries, type serialization. If a unit test needs infrastructure to run, it's testing the wrong thing. Integration tests run against real infrastructure. Every test gets its own ephemeral Postgres database, spun up and torn down automatically. They test that the actual SQL, the actual locks, the actual business rules, and the actual HTTP layer all behave correc…

items.sectionTitle: Unit tests - pure logic, zero infrastructure
items.snippet: Unit tests - pure logic, zero infrastructure Config validation - proves that misconfigured environment variables (min delay higher than max, malformed CORS URLs) crash the app at boot, not silently in production. Error mapping - proves every internal error variant maps to the correct HTTP status code and JSON shape. A database RowNotFound never leaks raw SQL text to the client. ValidatedJson extractor - proves the exact boundary between a 400 (broken JSON structure) and a 422 (valid JSON, failed business rules). Redis key formatting - proves idempotency keys are constructed correctly and HTTP header extraction works regardless of casing. Stage helpers - proves the predecessor/successor chain…

items.sectionTitle: Integration tests - real infrastructure, real behavior
items.snippet: Integration tests - real infrastructure, real behavior Database layer - proves the raw SQL works before anything else. FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED is tested directly. The session sweep logic is proven: one failed stage correctly flips the parent session to FAILED . Outbox relay - a scripted publisher simulates TCP hangs, publish timeouts, and corrupt payloads. Proves that when RabbitMQ rejects a publish, the Postgres transaction rolls back and the outbox row survives intact. A 10,000-row batch test proves the indexes keep queries under 5 seconds. HTTP layer - the full Axum router is wired with a live AppState . Tests bombard it with missing API keys (401), malformed JSON (400), and business valid…

items.sectionTitle: Stress tests - full cluster under load
items.snippet: Stress tests - full cluster under load Every verification session is launched at the exact same millisecond using Promise.allSettled - a deliberate thundering herd. This forces Postgres row locks, outbox micro-batching, and RabbitMQ routing to prove themselves under maximum contention simultaneously. The script mounts the Docker socket and acts as its own infrastructure monitor - querying real-time CPU, RAM, and network I/O for every container every 3 seconds. Network stats are delta-computed so the numbers reflect bandwidth during the test window specifically, not since the container booted. A slow response can be traced directly to a container hitting 100% CPU. Results are written to three…

items.snippet: Boot setup Both the API and worker call one initialization function at startup. Log level is controlled by RUST_LOG - defaults to info in production to keep log volume and cost low. Injecting RUST_LOG=info,app_core=debug at runtime enables deep traces for a specific crate without redeployment. Log format switches on environment. Local gets human-readable output with file names and line numbers - click a log line, jump to the code. Production gets compact JSON - lower CPU overhead, easy for log aggregators to parse.
items.searchText: tracing & observability boot setup  boot setup boot setup both the api and worker call one initialization function at startup. log level is controlled by rust_log - defaults to info in production to keep log volume and cost low. injecting rust_log=info,app_core=debug at runtime enables deep traces for a specific crate without redeployment. log format switches on environment. local gets human-readable output with file names and line numbers - click a log line, jump to the code. production gets compact json - lower cpu overhead, easy for log aggregators to parse.

items.snippet: Spans over log lines Everything is structured around spans, not individual log statements. A span covers a duration and carries context - session_id , stage , outbox_id . Every log event emitted inside a span automatically inherits that context. You don't pass variables through every function call; the span carries them.
items.searchText: tracing & observability spans over log lines  spans over log lines spans over log lines everything is structured around spans, not individual log statements. a span covers a duration and carries context - session_id , stage , outbox_id . every log event emitted inside a span automatically inherits that context. you don't pass variables through every function call; the span carries them.

items.snippet: trace_id on every API error Tower's trace middleware stamps a unique 16-character hex ID on every HTTP request. When the API returns a 4xx or 5xx, that ID is injected directly into the JSON error response. A customer pastes that ID into the log search and the exact database query, validation failure, and stack trace for their specific request appears instantly. No reproduction steps needed.
items.searchText: tracing & observability trace_id on every api error  trace_id on every api error trace_id on every api error tower's trace middleware stamps a unique 16-character hex id on every http request. when the api returns a 4xx or 5xx, that id is injected directly into the json error response. a customer pastes that id into the log search and the exact database query, validation failure, and stack trace for their specific request appears instantly. no reproduction steps needed.

items.snippet: Worker spans built manually Workers have no incoming HTTP request, so spans are constructed explicitly. The outbox relay extracts session_id and stage from the payload before publishing and attaches them to the span - a failed publish log reads "failed to publish Stage 2 for Session XYZ", not just "publish failed." The stage processor uses .instrument(span) on each async task in the JoinSet . Standard span entry would bleed log context across concurrently running jobs sharing the same thread. .instrument() binds the span to the specific future, not the thread - Job A's logs never mix with Job B's.
items.searchText: tracing & observability worker spans built manually  worker spans built manually worker spans built manually workers have no incoming http request, so spans are constructed explicitly. the outbox relay extracts session_id and stage from the payload before publishing and attaches them to the span - a failed publish log reads "failed to publish stage 2 for session xyz", not just "publish failed." the stage processor uses .instrument(span) on each async task in the joinset . standard span entry would bleed log context across concurrently running jobs sharing the same thread. .instrument() binds the span to the specific future, not the thread - job a's logs never mix with job b's.

items.snippet: Surgical field recording Instead of logging generic completion messages, specific counts are recorded directly onto the span - recovered jobs, permanently failed jobs. Log aggregators can graph these fields in real time without custom parsing rules.
items.searchText: tracing & observability surgical field recording  surgical field recording surgical field recording instead of logging generic completion messages, specific counts are recorded directly onto the span - recovered jobs, permanently failed jobs. log aggregators can graph these fields in real time without custom parsing rules.

items.snippet: Debug gate Expensive log operations - like printing a list of all published outbox IDs - are gated behind tracing::enabled!(Level::DEBUG) . At info level, that code path is never evaluated. Debug detail is available when needed without paying the cost in production.
